meta.author: Yusuke Yokota
meta.description: Centralized curriculum data for MATH-CS COMPASS - single source of truth for section pages and compass map

I.title: Linear Algebra to Algebraic Foundations
I.description: Master the transition from computational Linear Algebra to the rigorous world of Abstract Algebra. This section covers linear systems, vector spaces, and eigenvalues, before generalizing these concepts into the fundamental algebraic structures of Groups, Rings, and Fields.
I.tagline: From Vector Spaces to Universal Algebraic Structures

parts.title: Linear Equations
parts.tesseraMessage: Where every journey begins! Ax = b is the foundation of so much in CS.
parts.description: Master systems of linear equations: Gaussian elimination to RREF, the matrix equation Ax=b, linear combinations, span, linear independence, and the geometric structure of solution sets in homogeneous and nonhomogeneous form.

parts.title: Linear Transformation
parts.tesseraMessage: Transformations are how we move and reshape data. Graphics, ML, everywhere!
parts.description: Linear transformations as the structural maps of vector spaces: linearity, surjective/injective characterizations via column behavior, and the equivalence with matrix multiplication.

parts.title: Matrix Algebra
parts.tesseraMessage: Matrices are the language computers speak. Master this well!
parts.description: Matrix operations and structure: special matrices, invertibility and the 2×2 formula, elementary matrices, block-partitioned multiplication, and LU factorization as the prototype of matrix factorization.

parts.title: Determinants
parts.tesseraMessage: Determinants tell you if a matrix is invertible - one number, so much meaning.
parts.description: Determinants from cofactor expansion: multiplicativity, behavior under transpose and row operations, the geometric interpretation as volume scaling, Cramer's rule, the adjugate inverse formula, and the Invertible Matrix Theorem.

parts.title: Vector Spaces
parts.tesseraMessage: Vector spaces are where linear algebra becomes truly abstract and powerful.
parts.description: The abstract structure of vector spaces and subspaces: the null space and column space as the two fundamental subspaces attached to a matrix, bases as minimal spanning sets and the unique representation theorem, the coordinate mapping as a linear isomorphism between an abstract space and \(\mathbb{R}^n\), and the rank-nullity theorem unifying dimension across the column and null spaces.

parts.title: Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors
parts.tesseraMessage: Eigenvalues appear everywhere - from Google's PageRank to quantum mechanics!
parts.description: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors as the directions preserved by a linear transformation up to scaling: the eigenspace structure, the characteristic equation as the algebraic source of eigenvalues, similarity as the equivalence relation under change of basis, the diagonalization criterion via independent eigenvectors, and the extension to complex eigenvalues governing rotational dynamics.

parts.title: Orthogonality
parts.tesseraMessage: Orthogonality keeps things clean and independent. QR is essential!
parts.description: The geometry of inner-product spaces: the standard inner product on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) inducing norm, distance, and orthogonality, orthogonal complements pairing the row and column spaces, orthonormal bases as the linear-algebraic ideal that turns coordinate computation into inner products, the orthogonal projection as the best approximation in a subspace, and the Gram-Schmidt process producing the QR factorization at the heart of least-squares and numerical linear algebra.

parts.title: Least-Squares Problems
parts.tesseraMessage: When exact solutions don't exist, least squares finds the best approximation.
parts.description: Least-squares solutions to inconsistent systems: normal equations, the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse and minimum-norm characterization, and the connection to linear regression.

parts.title: Symmetry
parts.tesseraMessage: SVD is one of the most useful decompositions in all of applied mathematics!
parts.description: Symmetry as the structural condition that guarantees real spectra and orthogonal diagonalizability: the spectral theorem for symmetric matrices, quadratic forms classified by eigenvalue signs (positive/negative definite, indefinite, semidefinite), the singular value decomposition extending diagonalization to arbitrary rectangular matrices, the condition number as a quantitative measure of numerical stability, and the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse via SVD that solves rank-deficient least-squares problems.

parts.title: Trace & Norms
parts.tesseraMessage: Norms measure size, and different norms give different perspectives.
parts.description: The trace operator and its cyclic property, the hierarchy of vector and matrix norms (Frobenius, induced, spectral, nuclear, p-norms), and the abstraction into metric spaces — the foundational vocabulary for ML regularization and the bridge into Section II Analysis.

parts.title: Kronecker Product & Tensor
parts.tesseraMessage: Tensors power modern deep learning. This is where things get multidimensional!
parts.description: Vectorization as a bijection between matrices and column vectors, the Kronecker product and the vec-Kronecker identity, and tensors as the multilinear generalization that powers modern deep learning.

parts.title: Schur Complement & Woodbury Identity
parts.tesseraMessage: A clever set of identities that makes updating inverses and determinants efficient. Essential for online learning!
parts.description: Master efficient techniques for updating matrix inverses and determinants using Woodbury, Sherman-Morrison, and Matrix Determinant Lemma.

sections.name: Case Study: BFGS and Rank-2 Updates

parts.title: Stochastic Matrix
parts.tesseraMessage: Markov chains are everywhere - from web search to speech recognition.
parts.description: Stochastic matrices and the convention divide between row- and column-stochastic forms, the Markov chain dynamics they generate, the spectral radius bound, steady-state vectors, and the symmetry of doubly stochastic matrices.

parts.title: Graph Laplacians and Spectral Methods
parts.tesseraMessage: Graph Laplacians connect linear algebra to network analysis. Beautiful!
parts.description: The graph Laplacian as the discrete Dirichlet energy operator: positive semidefiniteness, the Fiedler vector and algebraic connectivity, Cheeger's inequality and the spectral gap, the Graph Fourier Transform, spectral clustering, and the spectral foundation of Graph Neural Networks.

parts.title: Intro to Abstract Algebra
parts.tesseraMessage: Welcome to abstract algebra! Groups reveal the hidden symmetry in mathematics.
parts.description: The transition from linear algebra to abstract algebra: the group axioms, Abelian and non-Abelian examples, modular arithmetic and the units U(n), the additive/multiplicative duality, subgroups and the one-step test, cyclic structure, and the center/centralizer pair.

sections.name: The Group of Units Modulo n
Centralizer of an element in a Group

parts.title: More Finite Groups
parts.tesseraMessage: Permutations and cyclic structures - the building blocks of cryptography.
parts.description: Cyclic groups as the simplest nontrivial group structure: the integers modulo \(n\) and their generators, the Fundamental Theorem of Cyclic Groups classifying all subgroups, the Euler phi function counting generators and elements of each order, and the parallel development of permutation groups — the symmetric groups \(S_n\) via cycle notation and the alternating groups \(A_n\) carved out by the parity of permutations.

parts.title: Structural Group Theory
parts.tesseraMessage: Isomorphisms show when different-looking structures are secretly the same.
parts.description: The structural toolkit for understanding groups up to relabeling: cosets partitioning a group into equal-sized blocks, Lagrange's theorem constraining subgroup orders to divide the group order, normal subgroups as the consistency condition that makes the coset partition itself a group (the factor group \(G/N\)), homomorphisms as structure-preserving maps with normal kernels, and isomorphisms identifying groups that are abstractly the same — culminating in inner automorphisms and the action of conjugation.

sections.name: Normal Subgroups: The Requirement for Consistency

parts.title: Classification of Finite Abelian Groups
keywords: Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups
parts.tesseraMessage: Every finite abelian group can be broken down - a beautiful classification!
parts.description: The classification of finite Abelian groups via direct products: external and internal constructions, the LCM rule for orders, the gcd cyclic criterion and its Chinese Remainder Theorem corollary, and the Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups proven through primary decomposition — culminating in an RSA / CRT-accelerated decryption application.

sections.name: Order of Elements in Direct Products
When Is a Direct Product Cyclic?
Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups

parts.title: The Architecture of Rings and Fields
parts.tesseraMessage: Rings and fields - the algebraic structures behind polynomials and numbers.
parts.description: The algebraic architecture of rings: subrings and the subring test, integral domains and the cancellation law, fields as the strongest closure structure, the characteristic of a ring, and the discrete-vs-continuous perspective that motivates the polynomial-ring and field-extension chapters ahead.

sections.name: Perspective: The Crossroads of Discrete and Continuous

parts.title: Ideals and Factor Rings
parts.tesseraMessage: Ideals are to rings what normal subgroups are to groups. Deep connections!
parts.description: Ideals as the ring-theoretic analog of normal subgroups: the ideal test, principal ideals, factor rings R/A, the prime/maximal characterizations (R/A integral domain ⟺ A prime; R/A field ⟺ A maximal), and the First Isomorphism Theorem for Rings.

parts.title: Polynomial Rings
parts.tesseraMessage: Polynomial rings power modern cryptography - from AES to post-quantum!
parts.description: Polynomial rings R[x] as the bridge from discrete algebra to computational cryptography: the inheritance of the integral-domain property, polynomial multiplication as discrete convolution, the Division Algorithm and the Remainder/Factor theorems, F[x] as a principal ideal domain, irreducibility tests including Gauss's Lemma and Eisenstein's criterion, and the algebraic foundation of AES and post-quantum cryptography.

parts.title: Hierarchies of Integrity
parts.tesseraMessage: Understanding where factorization holds - and where it breaks - is essential for designing robust computational systems.
parts.description: Deep dive into the hierarchy of integral domains, focusing on the uniqueness of factorization and its implications in computer science.

parts.title: Extension Fields
parts.tesseraMessage: When a field can't solve an equation, we build a bigger universe that can - this is the foundation of both cryptography and geometric deep learning.
parts.description: Extension fields as the construction that adds roots of polynomials to a base field: the Fundamental Theorem of Field Theory, the structural identity F(a) ≅ F[x]/⟨p(x)⟩, splitting fields, and the bifurcation into two paths — algebraic extensions toward Geometric Deep Learning, and finite fields toward cryptography and coding theory.

parts.title: Geometry of Symmetry
parts.tesseraMessage: From discrete clicks to continuous flow - this is where algebra meets geometry and robotics.
parts.description: Explore the visual geometry of symmetry groups, from discrete dihedral groups to continuous Lie groups SO(3) and SE(3).

sections.name: Special Orthogonal Group & Special Euclidean Group

parts.title: Algebraic Extensions
parts.tesseraMessage: Just like we track dimensions in vector spaces, the Tower Rule tracks dimensions in fields. It's the rigorous math behind data packing!
parts.description: Algebraic and transcendental extensions, the degree of an extension and the Tower Rule [E:F][F:K] = [E:K], the Primitive Element Theorem, and closure properties (finite implies algebraic, algebraic over algebraic is algebraic) — with a foreshadowing connection to Geometric Deep Learning.

parts.title: Finite Fields
parts.tesseraMessage: Finite fields bound algebra to discrete, computable universes. This is the math that keeps the internet secure!
parts.description: Discover the structure, classification, and construction of Finite Fields (Galois Fields) and their essential role in computer science and cryptography.

parts.title: Lie Groups: Matrix Groups and Continuous Symmetry
parts.tesseraMessage: From discrete to continuous symmetry — closed subgroups of GL(n) are the arena of Lie theory.
parts.description: Define matrix Lie groups as closed subgroups of GL(n), survey the classical groups (SO, SU, SE(3)), prove connectedness and compactness properties, and state Cartan's closed subgroup theorem.

parts.title: The Matrix Exponential
parts.tesseraMessage: The exponential map bridges infinitesimal symmetries and finite transformations — the engine of Lie theory.
parts.description: Define the matrix exponential, prove convergence and fundamental properties, derive Rodrigues' rotation formula for SO(3), and characterize one-parameter subgroups as matrix exponentials.

sections.name: The Exponential Maps into the Group
Connections: Robotics, Information Geometry, and Beyond

parts.title: Lie Algebras and the Lie Bracket
parts.tesseraMessage: The Lie algebra is the DNA of a Lie group — a vector space with a bracket encoding non-commutativity.
parts.description: Define the Lie algebra as the tangent space at the identity, prove closure under the commutator bracket, establish the Jacobi identity, compute the classical Lie algebras, and reveal the cross product as the Lie bracket of so(3).

sections.name: The Tangent Space at the Identity
Structure Constants and the Algebra of Rotations

parts.title: The Lie Correspondence
parts.tesseraMessage: The algebra determines the group locally — BCH, double covers, and the adjoint representation reveal how.
parts.description: Establish the Lie group-Lie algebra correspondence via the BCH formula, construct the SU(2)→SO(3) double cover and its quaternion interpretation, and define the adjoint representations Ad and ad.

sections.name: The Double Cover: SU(2) and SO(3)

II.title: Calculus to Optimization & Analysis
II.description: Master the transition from computational calculus to rigorous mathematical analysis. This section bridges multivariable calculus and optimization with the analytical blueprints of ML, including Taylor's theorem, convergence theory in metric spaces, and the measure-theoretic foundations of Lebesgue integration.
II.tagline: The Mathematics of Change and Convergence

parts.title: Linear Approximations
parts.tesseraMessage: The gradient points uphill. In ML, we usually go the opposite way!
parts.description: Linear approximation as the foundational strategy of multivariate calculus: the derivative as a linear operator, differentials and the \(o(\|\mathbf{h}\|)\) characterization of differentiability, vector derivatives via gradient and quadratic forms, and the role of norms as the bridge from finite-dimensional calculus to functional analysis.

parts.title: Jacobian
parts.tesseraMessage: The Jacobian is the backbone of backpropagation. Chain rule magic!
parts.description: The Jacobian matrix as the local linear transformation of vector-valued maps: its geometric interpretation as a hypervolume scaling factor, the multivariate chain rule, and the contrast between forward-mode and reverse-mode automatic differentiation that makes backpropagation feasible at neural-network scale.

parts.title: Matrix Calculus
parts.tesseraMessage: Matrix calculus - where derivatives get truly multidimensional.
parts.description: Differentiation of matrix-valued matrix functions \(F : \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \to \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\): the differential calculus of \(X^k\) and \(X^{-1}\), why no single \(\partial F / \partial X\) matrix exists due to non-commutativity, the differential of the LU decomposition, and the bridge from autodiff to differentiable linear algebra in modern ML pipelines.

sections.name: Derivative of the Square Matrix Functions
Derivative of the LU Decomposition Matrix

parts.title: Intro to Numerical Computation
parts.tesseraMessage: Theory meets practice! Understanding numerical errors is crucial for real code.
parts.description: Bridging analytical calculus and computer arithmetic: finite-difference approximations, the interplay between truncation and rounding error in floating-point arithmetic, principled choice of perturbation scale, and the practice of numerical validation as a check on analytical derivations.

parts.title: Scalar Functions of a Matrix
parts.tesseraMessage: Derivatives of determinants and traces - essential for advanced ML proofs.
parts.description: Scalar-valued functions on matrix space and their gradients: the Frobenius inner product as the natural pairing for differentiation, the gradient of the Frobenius norm and bilinear forms, Jacobi's formula \(d(\det X) = \det(X)\,\mathrm{tr}(X^{-1} dX)\), and applications in differentiable linear algebra and characteristic polynomial sensitivity.

parts.title: The Mean Value Theorem
parts.tesseraMessage: Taylor's theorem lets us approximate any smooth function locally. Powerful!
parts.description: The Mean Value Theorem and its descendants — Lagrange's MVT, Cauchy's generalization, Taylor's Theorem in Peano and Lagrange forms, and the higher-dimensional MVT — establishing the rigorous quantitative bridge between local linear approximation and global function behavior, with foreshadowing toward information geometry.

parts.title: Gradient Descent (First-order Method)
parts.tesseraMessage: Gradient descent is the workhorse of ML - simple yet powerful optimization.
parts.description: First-order optimization on convex and non-convex landscapes: convex sets and functions, the first- and second-order characterizations of convexity, gradient descent and its convergence behavior, stochastic and mini-batch SGD as the workhorse of modern ML, and subgradients for non-smooth objectives such as the \(L_1\) regularizer.

parts.title: Newton's method (Second-order Method)
parts.tesseraMessage: Newton's method: faster convergence using curvature. BFGS makes it practical!
parts.description: Second-order optimization beyond gradient descent: line search via the Armijo and Wolfe conditions, Newton's method and its quadratic convergence near minima, and the BFGS / L-BFGS quasi-Newton family that approximates the inverse Hessian via the secant condition — the practical bridge between scalable first-order methods and full second-order solvers.

parts.title: Constrained Optimization
parts.tesseraMessage: KKT conditions are the foundation of constrained optimization. Essential theory!
parts.description: Optimization under equality and inequality constraints: tangent spaces of constraint surfaces, the Lagrange multiplier rule and the Lagrangian construction, the active set and LICQ regularity, and the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions unifying primal feasibility, dual feasibility, stationarity, and complementary slackness — the foundation of duality theory and constrained ML problems.

parts.title: Riemann Integration
parts.tesseraMessage: Riemann integration - the classical approach before Lebesgue changed everything.
parts.description: Riemann integration via upper and lower sums, Darboux's criterion in the partition-norm formulation, and improper integration as a limiting procedure — together with the structural limitations exposed by the Dirichlet function that motivate the transition to measure theory and Lebesgue's integrability criterion.

parts.title: Measure Theory with Probability
parts.tesseraMessage: Measure theory makes probability rigorous. σ-algebras are everywhere!
parts.description: The measure-theoretic foundation of probability: \(\sigma\)-algebras as the structure of measurable events, measures and probability measures via countable additivity, continuity of measure, \(\sigma\)-finiteness, the Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(\mathbb{R}\), Lebesgue measure, and Carathéodory's Extension Theorem — the rigorous platform unifying probability and integration.

parts.title: Intro to Lebesgue Integration
parts.tesseraMessage: Lebesgue integration handles functions Riemann cannot. 'Almost everywhere' matters!
parts.description: Abstract integration with respect to a measure: measurable functions, characteristic and simple functions as the elementary building blocks, the integral constructed first for nonnegative measurable functions and then extended to general signed integrands, almost-everywhere equivalence, and the bridge from Riemann to Lebesgue integrability.

sections.name: The Integral of Finite Nonnegative Measurable Functions
The Integral of Nonnegative Measurable Functions
The Integral of General Measurable Functions

parts.title: Duality in Optimization & Analysis
parts.tesseraMessage: Duality gives us bounds and insights. Strong duality = same optimal value!
parts.description: Duality theory and quantitative analysis: the Lagrange dual function, weak and strong duality with Slater's condition, the duality gap as the bridge between primal and dual problems, and \(L\)-smoothness, Lipschitz continuity, and contractions as the regularity conditions governing convergence rates of iterative optimization.

parts.title: Fourier Series
parts.tesseraMessage: Fourier series decompose signals into frequencies. Music, images, everything!
parts.description: Fourier series as the orthogonal decomposition of periodic functions: orthogonality of trigonometric systems, real and complex exponential forms, Fourier coefficients, Parseval's identity as a discrete Plancherel theorem, and convergence properties — pointwise via Dirichlet–Jordan and the Gibbs phenomenon at jump discontinuities.

parts.title: Fourier Transform
parts.tesseraMessage: FFT changed the world - fast signal processing makes modern tech possible.
parts.description: The continuous Fourier transform on \(\mathbb{R}\): linearity, translation, scaling, and differentiation properties, Plancherel's theorem and Fourier inversion, the convolution theorem turning convolution into multiplication, and the discrete and fast variants (DFT, FFT) underpinning modern signal processing and Fourier neural operators.

parts.title: Foundations of Analysis: Metric Spaces
parts.tesseraMessage: Metric spaces abstract the notion of distance. The gateway to topology!
parts.description: Formalize the structure of metric spaces, covering distance, boundary, open and closed sets, topology, and completeness—the foundations ensuring optimization algorithms converge to valid solutions.

parts.title: Convergence & Boundedness
parts.tesseraMessage: Convergence is when the 'tail' of a sequence settles into an arbitrarily small ball. It's the structural anchor for every iterative process!
parts.description: Formalize convergence of sequences in metric spaces, establish the relationship between Cauchy sequences and completeness, and explore boundedness—the theoretical foundation for analyzing iterative algorithms.

parts.title: Continuity
parts.tesseraMessage: Lipschitz constants are the guardrails of AI, ensuring our gradients don't explode and algorithms actually converge.
parts.description: Formalize continuous and uniformly continuous functions between metric spaces, explore Lipschitz continuity, and understand why these properties ensure optimization algorithms behave predictably.

parts.title: Completeness
parts.tesseraMessage: Completeness ensures there are 'no holes' in our space—essential for knowing our algorithms will actually land somewhere!
parts.description: The full theory of complete metric spaces: the Cauchy criterion for completeness, the completion construction and its uniqueness, Cantor's Intersection Theorem characterizing completeness via nested sets, and the Banach Fixed-Point Theorem — the abstract guarantee that contraction mappings converge geometrically, underpinning iterative algorithms across analysis and ML.

parts.title: Connectedness
parts.tesseraMessage: If your feasible region isn't connected, you can't just 'walk' to the best solution. Topology is the ultimate map for our algorithms!
parts.description: Distinguish between spaces that are 'in one piece' versus 'fragmented,' and understand why this property underpins the Intermediate Value Theorem and modern clustering algorithms.

parts.title: Compactness
parts.tesseraMessage: Compactness is like a 'guarantee of existence.' It ensures that if you're looking for an optimal solution in a bounded search space, it's actually there to be found!
parts.description: Generalize the essential properties of closed and bounded intervals to metric spaces. Understand why compactness is the critical bridge between continuity and the existence of global maximums and minimums.

parts.title: Metric Equivalence
parts.tesseraMessage: Homeomorphisms are the 'rubber-sheet' maps of AI - they ensure that as we bend and stretch data into latent spaces, the fundamental connectivity remains unbroken.
parts.description: Formalize the concept of 'topological equivalence' using homeomorphisms. Explore the hierarchy of metric equivalence—topological, uniform, and Lipschitz—to understand when two spaces are essentially the same structure.

parts.title: Intro to Functional Analysis: Banach & Hilbert Spaces
parts.tesseraMessage: From finite vectors to infinite-dimensional functions. The ultimate mathematical bridge to advanced ML!
parts.description: The functional-analytic synthesis of linear algebra, topology, and measure theory: normed and Banach spaces, inner product and Hilbert spaces, the Jordan–von Neumann characterization via the parallelogram law, the \(L^p\) family including \(L^\infty\), and the dimensional dichotomy that separates finite-dimensional intuition from infinite-dimensional function spaces.

sections.name: What We Have Seen So Far...
Geometry of Unit Balls & ML Applications

parts.title: Bounded Linear Operators
parts.tesseraMessage: Operators are infinite-dimensional matrices! Their norm measures the maximum 'gain' — control it, and you control gradient flow.
parts.description: Bounded linear operators between normed spaces: the equivalence of boundedness and continuity, the operator norm and its equivalent formulations, completeness of the operator space \(\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y})\) when the codomain is Banach, Riesz's Lemma and the characterization of finite-dimensionality via compactness of the unit ball.

parts.title: Dual Spaces & Riesz Representation
parts.tesseraMessage: The derivative is not a vector — it's a covector! The inner product is just the dictionary translating between them.
parts.description: The topological dual space \(\mathcal{X}^*\) and its Banach completeness, the Riesz Representation Theorem identifying Hilbert spaces with their duals, the musical isomorphisms \(\sharp\) and \(\flat\) translating between vectors and covectors, the Hahn–Banach extension theorem, and the geometric reason that the gradient is fundamentally a covector — central to natural-gradient methods.

parts.title: Weak Topologies & Banach-Alaoglu
parts.tesseraMessage: In infinite dimensions, bounded doesn't mean compact — unless you weaken the topology. Banach-Alaoglu is the existence guarantee behind regularization.
parts.description: Introduce weak and weak* topologies to recover compactness in infinite-dimensional spaces, culminating in the Banach-Alaoglu theorem and its application to proving existence of optimal solutions in regularized machine learning.

parts.title: Spectral Theory of Compact Operators
parts.tesseraMessage: Compact operators are 'almost finite-dimensional' — the Spectral Theorem decomposes them into countable eigenmodes, just like SVD decomposes matrices.
parts.description: Generalize eigenvalue decomposition to infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces via compact operators, culminating in the Spectral Theorem and its applications to Functional PCA and kernel integral operators.

sections.name: Functional PCA & The Path to Kernels

parts.title: RKHS & Kernel Methods
parts.tesseraMessage: The kernel trick isn't a trick — it's the reproducing property of a Hilbert space. RKHS is where functional analysis meets machine learning.
parts.description: Construct Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces from positive definite kernels, prove Mercer's Theorem via spectral decomposition, and derive the Representer Theorem — unifying the entire Functional Analysis series into the mathematical foundation of kernel methods.

sections.name: Point Evaluation & The Need for RKHS

parts.title: Topological Spaces
parts.tesseraMessage: Topological spaces free geometry from the tyranny of distance — the axioms of open sets alone are enough to define continuity, convergence, and compactness without distance.
parts.description: Abstract away the metric to define topological spaces via open-set axioms. Develop bases, countability, Hausdorff separation, and the three fundamental constructions: subspace, product, and quotient topologies.

parts.title: Lᵖ Spaces — Construction & Inequalities
parts.tesseraMessage: From raw measurable functions to a normed space — the algebraic and metric scaffolding on which all of integration-based analysis is built.
parts.description: Constructs the Lp spaces as normed vector spaces: passes from measurable functions to equivalence classes modulo a.e. equality, defines the p-norm via the Lebesgue integral, and proves Young's, Hölder's, and Minkowski's inequalities. Establishes the algebraic and metric structure of Lp; completeness and the resulting Banach space structure are developed in the next chapter.

parts.title: Lᵖ Completeness & Convergence
parts.tesseraMessage: Completeness of Lp is the silent guarantee on which probability, signal processing, and quantum mechanics all rest.
parts.description: Proves the Riesz-Fischer theorem — that \(L^p\) is complete and hence a Banach space — via the convergence toolkit of Lebesgue integration (MCT, Fatou's lemma, DCT). Maps the four principal modes of convergence for measurable functions (\(L^p\), a.e., in measure, uniform a.e.), establishes their implications, and gives the canonical counterexamples (traveling bump, typewriter sequence) that block the missing arrows.

sections.name: Convergence in Lp — A Hierarchy of Modes
Why Complete Function Spaces Are Essential

III.title: Probability & Statistics
III.description: From combinatorial probability to measure-theoretic foundations: distributions and limit theorems, multivariate and exponential-family models, MLE and Bayesian inference, information theory and the Fisher geometry of statistical manifolds, Monte Carlo and importance sampling, Gaussian processes, and the Radon–Nikodym theorem that licenses the densities, scores, and divergences powering modern machine learning.
III.tagline: The Mathematics of Uncertainty and Inference

parts.title: Basic Probability Ideas
parts.tesseraMessage: Probability is the language of uncertainty. Bayes' theorem is your new best friend!
parts.description: Probability from the ground up: sample spaces and events, the Kolmogorov axioms over a σ-algebra \(\mathcal{F}\), counting via permutations and combinations, the inclusion–exclusion principle, conditional probability and independence, the Law of Total Probability, and Bayes' theorem as the inferential engine of machine learning.

parts.title: Random Variables
parts.tesseraMessage: Random variables turn randomness into mathematics we can compute with.
parts.description: Random variables as functions \(X: S \to \mathbb{R}\) (with a note on measurability foreshadowing the measure-theoretic treatment), the discrete/continuous dichotomy via PMFs, PDFs, and CDFs, expected value as the population mean, variance as a measure of spread, and the linearity of expectation.

parts.title: Gamma & Beta Distribution
parts.tesseraMessage: Gamma and Beta distributions model waiting times and proportions beautifully.
parts.description: The Gamma function as the analytic extension of the factorial, the Gamma distribution and its shape/rate parametrization, the Beta function and the Beta–Gamma identity, and the Beta distribution on \([0,1]\) — both serving as canonical conjugate priors that recur throughout Bayesian inference.

parts.title: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
parts.tesseraMessage: The Gaussian is everywhere! Central Limit Theorem explains why.
parts.description: The Gaussian integral and the normal distribution as the universal limiting law: the chi-squared distribution as a sum of squared standard normals, its derivation as a Gamma special case, and the Central Limit Theorem — the result that justifies Gaussian assumptions across statistics and machine learning.

parts.title: Student's t-Distribution
parts.tesseraMessage: Student's t handles small samples with grace. Heavy tails, robust inference.
parts.description: Heavy-tailed alternatives to the Gaussian: Student's \(t\)-distribution as a normal/chi ratio with explicit p.d.f. derivation and location–scale form, the Cauchy distribution as the \(t_1\) limit with no finite moments, the half-Cauchy as a weakly informative scale prior, and the Laplace distribution underlying \(\ell_1\) regularization.

parts.title: Covariance
parts.tesseraMessage: Covariance reveals how variables move together. PCA lives here!
parts.description: Joint distributions and the covariance \(\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]\), independence and its relation to zero covariance, the population covariance matrix and its positive semi-definiteness, and Principal Component Analysis derived both via the spectral decomposition of \(\Sigma\) and via the SVD of the centered data matrix.

parts.title: Correlation
parts.tesseraMessage: Correlation normalizes covariance - easier to interpret, same insight.
parts.description: Cross-covariance between random vectors and its sample estimator, the correlation coefficient as the scale-invariant standardization of covariance, its boundedness \(|\rho| \le 1\) via Cauchy–Schwarz, and the correlation matrix obtained by sandwiching \(\Sigma\) with the inverse standard-deviation diagonal — the dimensionless lens through which feature relationships are read.

parts.title: Multivariate Distributions
parts.tesseraMessage: Multivariate Gaussians are the foundation of Gaussian processes and more.
parts.description: Multivariate distributions for vector-valued data: the multivariate normal \(\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \Sigma)\) and the Mahalanobis distance, the Cholesky decomposition as the reparametrization trick that drives sampling and differentiable generative models, the Dirichlet distribution as the conjugate prior over the simplex, and the Wishart distribution over positive-definite matrices.

parts.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
parts.tesseraMessage: MLE finds the parameters that make your data most likely. Foundational!
parts.description: From data to parameters: point estimators with bias, variance, and the MSE decomposition, the likelihood function as a function of \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) given fixed data, and Maximum Likelihood Estimation via the log-likelihood — illustrated through closed-form derivations for the Binomial and Normal models that recur as building blocks throughout statistical learning.

parts.title: Statistical Inference & Hypothesis Testing
parts.tesseraMessage: Hypothesis testing helps us make decisions under uncertainty. Be careful with p-values!
parts.description: Frequentist and Bayesian inference side by side: null hypothesis significance testing with Type I/II errors and p-values, the \(t\)-test family for one- and two-sample mean comparisons, the categorical distinction between confidence intervals and credible intervals, and the bootstrap as a resampling alternative to analytic standard errors.

parts.title: Linear Regression
parts.tesseraMessage: Linear regression connects statistics to ML. Where prediction begins!
parts.description: Learn linear regression as a probabilistic model: the equivalence between MLE under Gaussian noise and ordinary least squares, normal equations, and connections to ridge/Lasso regularization.

parts.title: Entropy
parts.tesseraMessage: Entropy measures uncertainty - the heart of information theory.
parts.description: Information theory as the quantitative language of uncertainty: Shannon entropy and its joint, conditional, and cross variants, the chain rule of entropy, KL divergence as a directed measure of distributional discrepancy with non-negativity via Gibbs/Jensen, and mutual information as the symmetric quantity underlying feature selection, representation learning, and the cross-entropy loss.

parts.title: Convergence
parts.tesseraMessage: Convergence guarantees that our estimates improve. Math meets practice!
parts.description: Modes of convergence for sequences of random variables — almost sure, in probability, in \(L^p\), and in distribution — together with their strict hierarchy, the Strong and Weak Laws of Large Numbers, the Continuous Mapping Theorem, the Moment Generating Function and Lévy's continuity theorem, and the MGF-based proof of the Central Limit Theorem.

sections.name: Modes of Convergence & The Law of Large Numbers

parts.title: Intro to Bayesian Statistics
parts.tesseraMessage: Bayesian thinking updates beliefs with evidence. Prior → Posterior.
parts.description: Bayesian inference as the systematic update of belief: prior, likelihood, posterior, and the marginal evidence, the conjugate prior framework that makes posterior computation tractable, the interpretation of hyperparameters as pseudo-counts, the univariate normal model with sequential online updates, and posterior predictive distributions for anomaly detection and uncertainty quantification.

parts.title: The Exponential Family
parts.tesseraMessage: The exponential family unifies many distributions elegantly. Beautiful structure!
parts.description: The exponential family in canonical form \(p(x \mid \boldsymbol{\eta}) = h(x)\exp(\boldsymbol{\eta}^\top T(x) - A(\boldsymbol{\eta}))\): natural parameters and sufficient statistics, the log-partition function \(A(\boldsymbol{\eta})\) as a cumulant generator, minimal representations, and MLE via moment matching — the unifying structure behind nearly every standard distribution and the foundation for generalized linear models.

parts.title: Fisher Information Matrix
parts.tesseraMessage: Fisher information measures how much data tells us about parameters.
parts.description: The Fisher Information Matrix as the curvature of the log-likelihood: the score function and its zero-mean property, the equivalence \(F(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\mathbb{E}[\nabla^2 \log p]\) (distinguished from the Hessian by reparametrization invariance via Čencov's theorem), natural gradient descent on the statistical manifold, the Cramér-Rao lower bound, and Jeffreys prior as the invariant uninformative prior.

parts.title: Bayesian Decision Theory
parts.tesseraMessage: Decision theory tells us how to act optimally under uncertainty.
parts.description: Decision-making under uncertainty: posterior expected loss and Bayes estimators, the canonical trio of squared-error/absolute-error/zero-one losses yielding the posterior mean, median, and mode (MAP), frequentist risk and admissibility, the Bayes-risk equivalence theorem, the minimax criterion, and the classifier diagnostics — confusion matrix, ROC, EER, PR curves — that operationalize these ideas under class imbalance.

sections.name: Continuous Parameters: Squared and Absolute Error Loss
Frequentist Risk, Bayes Risk, and Minimax

parts.title: Markov Chains
keywords: Maximum likelihood estimation(MLE) in Markov models
parts.tesseraMessage: Markov chains model sequences - from text to weather to DNA.
parts.description: Probabilistic graphical models with a focus on Markov chains: Bayesian networks and their factorization theorem, the first-order Markov property, n-gram language models as a parameter-estimation case study, MLE for transition probabilities, and Dirichlet smoothing as the Bayesian remedy for sparse-data zero counts — the algorithmic bridge to modern sequence modeling.

sections.name: A Code Sample (MLE in Markov Models)

parts.title: Monte Carlo Methods
parts.tesseraMessage: Monte Carlo turns random sampling into computational power!
parts.description: Monte Carlo as the universal numerical strategy when expectations resist closed-form evaluation: the Monte Carlo estimator with its consistency and \(O(N^{-1/2})\) convergence rate, the Glivenko–Cantelli theorem as the empirical-distribution backbone, credible intervals and Highest Posterior Density regions, and Markov Chain Monte Carlo as the workhorse for sampling from intractable posteriors.

sections.name: Demo: Monte Carlo for Credible Intervals

parts.title: Importance Sampling
parts.tesseraMessage: Importance sampling focuses computation where it matters most.
parts.description: Importance sampling as a change of measure for expectation estimation: importance weights and the variance of the direct estimator, the bias–consistency tradeoff of self-normalized importance sampling, the effective sample size as a diagnostic for weight degeneracy, and Annealed Importance Sampling for high-dimensional targets — the foundation for RLHF, off-policy reinforcement learning, and variational inference.

parts.title: Gaussian Processes
parts.tesseraMessage: GPs give uncertainty estimates for free. Bayesian nonparametrics at its finest!
parts.description: Gaussian Processes as distributions over functions: the GP definition via consistent finite-dimensional Gaussian marginals, Mercer kernels as the symmetric positive-definite functions that determine the covariance structure, and closed-form GP regression with calibrated predictive uncertainty — the canonical nonparametric Bayesian model and a continuous-domain counterpart to kernel methods.

parts.title: Measure- Theoretic Probability
parts.tesseraMessage: Discrete sums and continuous integrals were never two operations — they were one integral in disguise, waiting for the right measure.
parts.description: Translates the core vocabulary of probability into measure theory: random variables become measurable functions, distributions become pushforward measures, and expectations become Lebesgue integrals. Unifies discrete, continuous, and mixed distributions under a single framework.

parts.title: Limit Theorems & Product Measures
parts.tesseraMessage: When can you push a limit inside an expectation? And what does 'independent' truly mean? The answers live in product measures and the three great limit theorems.
parts.description: Completes the measure-theoretic foundation for probability: translates the Monotone Convergence Theorem, Fatou's Lemma, and the Dominated Convergence Theorem into probabilistic language, then formalizes independence via σ-algebras and product measures, giving 'i.i.d. sampling' its mathematical content through Fubini's theorem.

parts.title: Signed Measures & the Radon-Nikodym Theorem
parts.tesseraMessage: Every density, every likelihood ratio, every change of measure — all are one derivative in disguise. Section II's Hilbert-space machinery delivers, in a single proof, the foundation underneath modern probabilistic ML.
parts.description: Proves the Radon-Nikodym theorem via von Neumann's L² method, after establishing signed measures, the Jordan-Hahn decomposition, absolute continuity, and the ε-δ characterization. Closes three retroactive accounts (PDF existence in prob-22, L^p duality in calc-25, conditional expectation preview in prob-23) and licenses the densities, scores, importance weights, and KL divergences that underpin Bayesian inference, diffusion models, and RLHF.

IV.title: Discrete Mathematics & Algorithms
IV.description: Explore the foundations of discrete mathematics and algorithms, covering graph theory, combinatorics, and the theory of computation. Learn key concepts essential for mathematical reasoning and algorithmic problem-solving.
IV.tagline: The Mathematics of Logic and Finite Structures

parts.title: Intro to Graph Theory
parts.tesseraMessage: Graphs are everywhere - social networks, molecules, the internet itself!
parts.description: Foundations of graph theory: graphs, degrees, isomorphism, subgraphs, paths, cycles, and trees.

parts.title: Intro to Combinatorics
keywords: Sum of powers of positive integers
parts.tesseraMessage: Counting cleverly is an art. Combinatorics powers probability and CS alike.
parts.description: Foundational counting techniques: the rule of sum and product, permutations and combinations, binomial and multinomial coefficients, Pascal's triangle, Chu's theorem, and sums of powers of integers derived via the Pascal matrix.

parts.title: Intro to Theory of Computation
parts.tesseraMessage: Automata recognize patterns. The simplest model of computation!
parts.description: Finite automata (deterministic and nondeterministic), regular expressions, and their equivalence — three models that all describe exactly the regular languages.

parts.title: Boolean Logic
parts.tesseraMessage: True or false, 0 or 1 - Boolean logic is how computers think.
parts.description: Boolean logic operates on the two values \{0, 1\} via fundamental operations — negation, conjunction, disjunction, and their derivatives (XOR, NAND, NOR). We define Boolean functions as maps \{0,1\}^n → \{0,1\}, prove that {¬, ∧, ∨} is functionally complete via disjunctive normal form, and deduce that NAND alone (and NOR alone) suffices. We close with Boolean circuits as DAGs, gateways to circuit complexity.

parts.title: Context-Free Languages
parts.tesseraMessage: Context-free grammars define programming languages. You use them every day!
parts.description: Defines context-free grammars syntactically and via the derivation relation that determines the language they generate. Introduces the pumping lemma for regular languages, applies it to prove 0^n 1^n is not regular, then formalizes pushdown automata and the equivalence between CFGs and PDAs. Closes with the pumping lemma for context-free languages and the strict hierarchy of regular, deterministic context-free, and context-free languages, separated by 0^n 1^n and the palindrome language.

parts.title: Turing Machines
parts.tesseraMessage: Turing machines define what's computable. Some problems have no algorithm!
parts.description: Defines the Turing machine as the standard model of computation, formalizing configurations, the yields relation, and acceptance. Introduces Turing-recognizability and Turing-decidability, the Church-Turing thesis, and the decidability hierarchy bridging context-free, decidable, and recognizable languages. Concludes with Cantor's diagonalization method to prove the existence of languages beyond the reach of any Turing machine.

parts.title: Time Complexity
parts.tesseraMessage: Big-O notation tells you how algorithms scale. Essential for interviews!
parts.description: Develops asymptotic analysis through Big-O and little-o notation, then defines time complexity classes via the running time of deterministic Turing machines. Compares single-tape and multitape models on the language $\{0^k 1^k\}$, proving that the choice of machine model affects time complexity by at most a quadratic factor. Concludes with the class $P$ of polynomial-time decidable languages, motivating the polynomial-vs-exponential distinction central to complexity theory.

parts.title: Eulerian & Hamiltonian
parts.tesseraMessage: Can you traverse every edge? Every vertex? Different questions, different answers.
parts.description: Two superficially similar graph problems with radically different computational fates: Eulerian circuits (visiting every edge exactly once) admit a polynomial-time degree-parity test from Euler's 1736 result, while Hamiltonian cycles (visiting every vertex exactly once) are NP-complete with no known polynomial algorithm — a striking concrete instance of the P-vs-NP boundary that motivates the theory of computation track. Adjacency matrices and adjacency lists are introduced as the standard graph representations underlying any algorithmic treatment.

parts.title: Class NP
parts.tesseraMessage: P vs NP - the million-dollar question! Is checking always easier than solving?
parts.description: NP captures problems whose solutions can be verified in polynomial time. We prove the equivalence of verifier and nondeterministic-Turing-machine characterizations, establish the chain P ⊆ NP ⊆ EXPTIME, and develop polynomial-time reductions and NP-completeness — making precise the Clay Millennium P vs NP question. We close with the practical response to intractability: approximation, heuristics, and SAT/SMT solvers.

parts.title: Network Flow & Walks on Graphs
parts.tesseraMessage: Shortest paths, maximum flow, random walks — three lenses on how graphs move things from A to B.
parts.description: Three lenses on graph dynamics: (1) shortest paths via greedy Dijkstra and dynamic-programming Bellman-Ford with negative-cycle detection; (2) maximum flow via Ford-Fulkerson augmentation and the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem as a three-way equivalence with LP-duality interpretation; (3) random walks with the closed-form stationary distribution \(\pi_v = \deg(v)/(2m)\), spectral mixing-time bounds, and Lovász's commute-time formula. Applications include currency arbitrage, image segmentation via graph cuts, PageRank, and graph representation learning.

parts.title: Trees & Enumeration
parts.tesseraMessage: How many trees are there? Catalan, Schröder, and Cayley reveal the surprising answers.
parts.description: Trees as data structures and combinatorial objects: BSTs with O(h) operations, the Ω(n log n) sorting bound, Catalan enumeration of binary trees via Dyck paths and reflection, and Cayley's n^{n-2} formula via the Prüfer bijection.

parts.title: Planar Graphs & Euler's Formula
parts.tesseraMessage: Can you draw it without crossings? Euler's formula reveals the deep structure of planar graphs.
parts.description: Study planar graphs, Euler's formula V - E + F = 2, Kuratowski's and Wagner's characterization theorems, dual graphs, surface embeddings, and the Five Color Theorem.

parts.title: Incidence Structure & Cycle/Cut Spaces
parts.tesseraMessage: Assign directions to edges, and the whole of linear algebra comes to life on your graph.
parts.description: Study the oriented incidence matrix, discrete gradient and divergence, the cycle space and cut space decomposition, and the factorization L = BBᵀ of the graph Laplacian.

parts.title: Simplicial Complexes
parts.tesseraMessage: Vertices, edges, triangles, tetrahedra — simplicial complexes generalize graphs into every dimension.
parts.description: Study abstract simplicial complexes, Vietoris-Rips and Čech constructions from data, the f-vector and Euler characteristic, oriented simplices, the boundary operator ∂ₖ, and the fundamental property ∂² = 0.

parts.title: Intro to Homology
keywords: Every boundary is a cycle (Bₖ ⊆ Zₖ)
H₁ for graphs: Z₁ / {0} = ker ∂₁ (cycle rank recovery)
Solid tetrahedron Δ³: (β₀, β₁, β₂, β₃) = (1, 0, 0, 0)
Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 as special case (sphere)
Birth and death of homology classes
parts.tesseraMessage: Not every cycle bounds something. The gap between cycles and boundaries — that's where the holes live.
parts.description: Define cycle groups Zₖ = ker ∂ₖ and boundary groups Bₖ = im ∂_{k+1}, construct the homology groups Hₖ = Zₖ / Bₖ, compute Betti numbers for the hollow and solid tetrahedra and the torus, prove the Euler-Poincaré theorem via telescoping rank-nullity, and connect to persistent homology and the discrete Hodge theorem.

sections.name: Chain Groups and the Chain Complex

V.title: Machine Learning
V.description: The grand synthesis of the MATH-CS COMPASS curriculum. Move from theory to implementation by exploring how high-dimensional vectors are optimized across manifolds using statistical learning theory. This section integrates algebraic structure, calculus-based optimization, and probabilistic inference to decode the underlying mathematical 'physics' of artificial intelligence.
V.tagline: The Mathematics of Synthesis and Intelligence

parts.title: Intro to Machine Learning
parts.tesseraMessage: Welcome to ML! This is where all the math comes together beautifully.
parts.description: The conceptual foundation of machine learning as the search for a function \(f : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}\) from finite data: the three learning paradigms (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement), the canonical task taxonomy (regression, classification, density estimation, structured prediction), and the standard ML pipeline from data preprocessing through model evaluation.

parts.title: Regularized Regression
parts.tesseraMessage: Regularization prevents overfitting - the bias-variance tradeoff in action!
parts.description: Regularization as the principled response to overfitting in linear regression: Ridge regression with the squared \(\ell_2\) penalty and its closed-form shrinkage estimator, cross-validation (K-fold and LOOCV) for hyperparameter selection, the bias-variance decomposition that explains why regularization helps, and Lasso regression with the \(\ell_1\) penalty for automatic feature selection.

parts.title: Intro to Classification
parts.tesseraMessage: Classification draws boundaries in feature space. Kernels bend those boundaries!
parts.description: Classification as the search for a decision boundary in feature space: binary logistic regression via the sigmoid and the convex cross-entropy objective, the kernel trick that lifts linear classifiers to nonlinear feature maps through positive definite kernels, the RBF kernel and its Random Fourier Features approximation, and the multinomial extension via the softmax function.

parts.title: Neural Networks Basics
parts.tesseraMessage: Neural networks learn features automatically. Layers upon layers of abstraction!
parts.description: The multilayer perceptron as the foundational deep learning architecture: the layered composition \(\mathbf{x} \mapsto h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})\) of affine maps and elementwise nonlinearities, the role of activation functions and why ReLU supplanted sigmoid (vanishing gradients), training via empirical risk minimization with mini-batch SGD and backpropagation, and the hardware-driven rise of deep learning through GPUs.

parts.title: Automatic Differentiation
parts.tesseraMessage: Autodiff computes gradients automatically. The engine behind deep learning!
parts.description: Automatic differentiation as the algorithmic engine of deep learning: the computational graph as the data structure that decomposes a function into elementary operations, the contrast between forward-mode (Jacobian-vector products) and reverse-mode (vector-Jacobian products) and why the latter scales to neural networks with millions of parameters, a worked analytic example tracing the adjoint through the graph, and a PyTorch implementation.

parts.title: Support Vector Machine (SVM)
parts.tesseraMessage: SVMs find the widest margin. Elegant geometry meets optimization!
parts.description: Support Vector Machines as the geometric formulation of binary classification: the hard-margin primal problem maximizing the distance between the separating hyperplane and the nearest data points, the dual problem derived via Lagrange multipliers and the KKT conditions which exposes a sparse support-vector representation, the kernel trick that enables nonlinear decision boundaries through positive definite kernels, and the soft-margin extension via slack variables for non-separable data.

parts.title: PCA & Autoencoders
parts.tesseraMessage: PCA finds the directions of maximum variance. Autoencoders learn them!
parts.description: Linear and nonlinear dimensionality reduction unified through reconstruction: PCA as the variance-maximizing orthogonal projection derived from the spectral theorem applied to the covariance matrix, Kernel PCA extending this to nonlinear feature spaces via the Gram matrix and the double centering trick, autoencoders generalizing the encoder-decoder architecture to arbitrary neural networks, denoising autoencoders implicitly learning the score function \(\nabla_x \log p(x)\), and the manifold hypothesis as the geometric viewpoint motivating the upcoming smooth-manifold series.

parts.title: Clustering
parts.tesseraMessage: Clustering finds structure without labels. K-means is just the beginning!
parts.description: Unsupervised partitioning of data through three complementary lenses: K-means as iterative distortion minimization with K-means++ smart initialization, vector quantization reframing K-means as a learned codebook for signal compression, and spectral clustering exploiting the bottom eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian — connecting the discrete combinatorial cut problem to the continuous Dirichlet energy via Cheeger's inequality.

parts.title: Intro to Deep Neural Networks
parts.tesseraMessage: Transformers changed everything - attention is all you need!
parts.description: Modern deep learning architectures viewed through the lens of inductive bias and symmetry: convolutional networks as translation-equivariant feature extractors, residual connections and layer normalization as the techniques that enable training very deep networks, the attention mechanism culminating in self-attention and Multi-Head Attention as the building block of the Transformer and modern large language models, and an overarching reading of these architectures as instances of equivariant design — the entry point to geometric deep learning.

parts.title: Intro to Reinforcement Learning
parts.tesseraMessage: RL learns from interaction. Games, robotics, and beyond!
parts.description: Reinforcement learning as the framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty: the agent-environment loop formalized as a Markov Decision Process, value functions and Q-functions characterized by the Bellman equations, the exploration-exploitation tradeoff and ε-greedy strategies, and the algorithmic spectrum from dynamic programming (Value/Policy Iteration) through Monte Carlo and Temporal Difference methods (Q-Learning, SARSA) to modern policy gradient and actor-critic methods (REINFORCE, PPO) that power applications from robotics to RLHF for large language models.

parts.title: Natural Gradient Descent
parts.tesseraMessage: The natural gradient respects the manifold's curvature. Geometry guides optimization!
parts.description: Learn how the Fisher Information Matrix defines a Riemannian metric on parameter space, why Euclidean gradients are suboptimal on curved manifolds, and how K-FAC scales natural gradient methods to deep neural networks.

sections.name: The Geometry of Information: Interactive GMM Manifold

parts.title: Variational Autoencoder
parts.tesseraMessage: VAEs map data into probabilistic latent spaces. The bridge between deep learning and Bayesian reasoning!
parts.description: Variational Autoencoders as the synthesis of Bayesian inference and deep generative modeling: variational inference replacing intractable posterior computation with optimization over a tractable family, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) and its decomposition into reconstruction and KL regularization terms, the reparameterization trick enabling end-to-end gradient flow through stochastic latent variables, and the use of posterior variance as a quantitative proxy for epistemic uncertainty in robotic manipulation.
